current_focus: Track a Pareto frontier across reconstruction quality and sparsity/cost. Treat K=128 as one anchor point, not the only success criterion. Prefer experiments that add non-dominated tradeoff points, including smaller-K runs that accept some FVU increase when they improve the overall frontier.

failure_patterns.pattern: After two consecutive K128 loss-path probes (`AUXK_ALPHA=0.0625` and `DEAD_FEATURE_THRESHOLD=100000`) both failed to beat the declared `topk/signum/LR=1.6e-3/K=128/EF=12` anchor, the most useful single question is whether the exact reference recipe itself still reproduces a clean late-phase descent under the current stack. This round makes no recipe change; it reruns the fully explicit mainline baseline to refresh the curve-shape reference before any further LR or optimizer decisions.

topk.design_hypothesis: After two consecutive K128 loss-path probes (`AUXK_ALPHA=0.0625` and `DEAD_FEATURE_THRESHOLD=100000`) both failed to beat the declared `topk/signum/LR=1.6e-3/K=128/EF=12` anchor, the most useful single question is whether the exact reference recipe itself still reproduces a clean late-phase descent under the current stack. This round makes no recipe change; it reruns the fully explicit mainline baseline to refresh the curve-shape reference before any further LR or optimizer decisions.
next_steps: If the clean baseline reproduces the anchor cleanly, the next mainline question should return to a core axis, starting with a narrow LR check or an optimizer comparison per policy order.
If the clean baseline comes in materially worse or with a different curve shape, pause further recipe interpretation and treat baseline reproducibility as the immediate issue.
If the clean baseline unexpectedly improves, promote the refreshed explicit recipe as the operative anchor before any further exploration.

recent_rounds.hypothesis: The recent `signum` LR refinements did not convert into an accepted frontier update, so the next clean question is whether the current `topk` mainline is slightly over-regularized rather than under-tuned on step size. This round keeps the exact reference backbone `topk/signum/K128/EF12/LR=1.6e-3` fixed and changes only `AUXK_ALPHA`, reducing it from `0.03125` to `0.015625` to test whether weaker auxiliary pressure improves late-stage reconstruction without destabilizing the curve.

Lowering `AUXK_ALPHA` on the accepted `topk/signum/K128/EF12/LR=1.6e-3` recipe did not improve FVU, which suggests the current K128 recipe is close to saturated on the loss-shaping axis. This round changes only `K`, dropping from `128` to `64` while keeping the same family, optimizer, LR, and EF fixed, to test whether LUTurbo can gain a materially cheaper decomposition point with still-acceptable reconstruction quality on the current best-understood topk recipe.

Round 15 established `topk/signum/LR=1.6e-3/EF=12` at `K=64` as a valid new Pareto point, so the next most informative single-variable question is whether LUTurbo can push cost lower again without collapsing reconstruction. This round changes only `K`, moving from `64` to `32` while keeping the same family and recipe fixed, to measure the next step on the cost-quality frontier.

Round 16 showed that `topk/signum/LR=1.6e-3/EF=12` at `K=32` did not collapse and still maintained a smooth late-stage FVU descent, so the next single question is whether LUTurbo can push cost one more notch lower before quality degradation becomes too steep. This round changes only `K`, moving from `32` to `24` while keeping the same family and recipe fixed, to measure the next deployable point on the low-K cost-quality frontier.

Round 17 showed that `topk/signum/LR=1.6e-3/EF=12` at `K=24` still descended smoothly through the late phase rather than flattening early, so the current recipe appears capacity-limited but still stable. This round changes only `K`, moving from `24` to `16`, to answer whether LUTurbo can reach the next allowed lower-cost point before reconstruction degradation becomes clearly impractical.

Round 18 showed that `topk/signum/LR=1.6e-3/EF=12` at `K=16` still maintained a negative late-phase FVU slope instead of fully stalling, even though dead-feature pressure rose sharply. This round changes only `K`, moving from `16` to `8`, to answer whether the current export-compatible topk recipe can still support one more major cost reduction before reconstruction quality and compensation burden become clearly impractical.

Round 19 showed that `topk/signum/LR=1.6e-3/EF=12` at `K=8` suffered a large quality drop and much heavier dead-feature pressure, with the late curve nearly flattening but not fully collapsing. This round changes only `K`, moving from `8` to `4`, to answer the single remaining boundary question: whether the current export-compatible topk recipe has any usable signal left at the absolute minimum allowed K, or whether `K=8` already marks the effective floor.

Round 20 showed that `topk/signum/LR=1.6e-3/EF=12` at `K=4` is effectively past the practical floor: the late-phase FVU curve flattened completely while dead-feature pressure rose to about `0.68`, so further low-K descent is no longer the right question. This round changes only `K`, moving from the quality anchor side to `K=96`, to test whether LUTurbo can obtain a materially cheaper but still high-quality deployment point that may be more practical than the stressed ultra-low-K regime.

Round 21 showed that `topk/signum/LR=1.6e-3/EF=12` at `K=96` is a promising high-quality reduced-cost point: its curve kept descending smoothly through the late phase, and both `dead_pct` and `auxk` stayed very low. This round changes only the loss-shaping axis, lowering `AUXK_ALPHA` from `0.03125` to `0.015625` while keeping the `K=96` recipe fixed, to answer whether the new near-anchor deployment point is slightly over-regularized and can recover a bit more reconstruction quality without destabilizing training.
recent_rounds.termination_reason: 当前实际改动主轴是 k，primary_variable 不应写 other_param

recent_rounds.hypothesis: Round 14 showed that lowering `AUXK_ALPHA` below the `topk/signum/K128/EF12/LR=1.6e-3` reference did not improve FVU, which leaves the opposite loss-shaping question open: the current mainline may be slightly under-regularized rather than over-regularized. This round changes only `AUXK_ALPHA`, increasing it from `0.03125` to `0.0625` while keeping the exact K128 reference recipe fixed, to test whether stronger dead-feature pressure improves late-stage reconstruction or curve stability.

Round 23 showed that increasing `AUXK_ALPHA` above the `topk/signum/K128/EF12/LR=1.6e-3` reference did not improve the anchor recipe, which suggests the mainline is likely saturated on auxiliary-loss magnitude itself. This round changes only `DEAD_FEATURE_THRESHOLD`, lowering it from the effectively inactive `10000000` to `100000`, to test whether earlier dead-feature rescue improves late-stage reconstruction or curve shape without changing the core topk recipe.

After two consecutive K128 loss-path probes (`AUXK_ALPHA=0.0625` and `DEAD_FEATURE_THRESHOLD=100000`) both failed to beat the declared `topk/signum/LR=1.6e-3/K=128/EF=12` anchor, the most useful single question is whether the exact reference recipe itself still reproduces a clean late-phase descent under the current stack. This round makes no recipe change; it reruns the fully explicit mainline baseline to refresh the curve-shape reference before any further LR or optimizer decisions.

recent_insights: NOTE: Round 15 added a credible `K=64` Pareto point on the accepted topk/signum/LR1.6e-3/EF12 backbone.
NOTE: Round 16 continues the same low-K mapping by changing only `K` from 64 to 32.

NOTE: Judge this run on frontier usefulness, not raw comparison to K128 alone: the key question is whether `K=32` remains deployably compatible with a tolerable quality drop for the lower online selection and summation cost.
r16 topk param_only k32 -> keep fvu=0.24942675232887268
NOTE: Round 15 (`K=64`) reached `FVU=0.19926`; round 16 (`K=32`) reached `FVU=0.24943`, so the low-K frontier is still valid but degradation is now material.

NOTE: The `K=32` run kept a smooth late descent instead of flattening early, which suggests the recipe is capacity-limited rather than unstable and justifies one finer K step before declaring the boundary.
NOTE: For LUTurbo, this lane is valuable because it directly probes lower online selection and weighted-sum cost under a fixed export-compatible topk recipe.
r17 topk param_only k24 -> keep fvu=0.2722620368003845
NOTE: Round 17 (`K=24`) reached `FVU=0.2722620368003845`, a modest degradation from `K=32`, and its late-phase slope remained negative instead of collapsing into an early plateau.

NOTE: Compared with `K=32`, the `K=24` run showed higher `dead_pct` and `auxk` pressure in the late phase, suggesting capacity is getting tight, but the training dynamics still looked stable enough to justify one more K-only step.
NOTE: For LUTurbo, this is still the most direct way to probe lower online selection and weighted-sum cost while staying within the same export-compatible topk recipe.
r18 topk param_only k16 -> keep fvu=0.3084372282028198
NOTE: Round 18 (`K=16`) reached `FVU=0.3084372282028198`; the late-phase slope remained negative, so training was still improving rather than collapsing early.
NOTE: At `K=16`, `dead_pct` and `auxk` rose sharply in the late phase, indicating meaningful capacity stress and likely growing reliance on online compensation if K is reduced further.

NOTE: For LUTurbo, probing `K=8` is now the most informative single-variable action because it directly tests whether the current topk recipe still offers a viable lower-cost deployment point or whether `K=16` is already near the practical floor.
r19 topk param_only k8 -> keep fvu=0.380331814289093
NOTE: Round 19 (`K=8`) reached `FVU=0.380331814289093`, a large degradation from `K=16`, while late-phase improvement became very small.
NOTE: At `K=8`, late `dead_pct` rose to about `0.39` and `auxk` stayed high at about `0.37`, indicating strong capacity stress and likely growing online compensation burden.
NOTE: Even though the late curve nearly flattened at `K=8`, it did not fully collapse, so running `K=4` is the cleanest way to locate the hard lower boundary of the current topk/signum/EF12 recipe.
r20 topk param_only k4 -> keep fvu=0.4627891778945923
NOTE: Round 20 (`K=4`) appears to mark the hard lower bound of the current topk/signum regime: late improvement vanished while `dead_pct` climbed to about `0.68` and `auxk` remained around `0.58`.
NOTE: The current useful low-K region is likely above `K=8`, with `K=16` looking much more plausible than `K=8` or `K=4` for deployment-aligned tradeoffs.

NOTE: Given the hard low-end boundary is now mapped, the next most informative single-variable K question is whether `K=96` provides a stronger quality-cost sweet spot between the existing `K=64` and `K=128` points.
r21 topk param_only k96 -> keep fvu=0.1718563437461853
NOTE: Round 21 established `K=96` as a strong high-quality reduced-cost point: late-phase FVU kept descending smoothly and both `dead_pct` and `auxk` remained near zero.
NOTE: Because `K=96` is not capacity-stressed like the low-K regime, it is a better place than `K=8` or `K=16` to test whether the current auxiliary dead-feature pressure is slightly too strong.
NOTE: This round returns from K mapping to recipe tuning, while still staying inside the same family and on a deployment-relevant point rather than retreating all the way to the K128 anchor.
r22 topk param_only k? -> policy_reject (当前实际改动主轴是 k，primary_variable 不应写 other_param)

NOTE: Round 22 was policy-rejected because the proposed `AUXK_ALPHA` change was coupled with `K=96` relative to the validator's mainline reference; non-core recipe tests need to stay on the exact reference backbone to remain isolatable.
NOTE: Round 14 already tested weaker auxiliary pressure on the K128 mainline and did not improve FVU, so the remaining clean loss-axis question is whether stronger auxiliary pressure helps instead.

NOTE: This round is judged by full curve shape as well as terminal FVU: if stronger `AUXK_ALPHA` smooths late training or improves reconstruction, the current baseline was slightly under-regularized; if not, the loss axis is likely saturated on the K128 recipe.
r23 topk param_only k128 -> archive fvu=0.15255779027938843
NOTE: Round 14 (`AUXK_ALPHA=0.015625`) and Round 23 (`AUXK_ALPHA=0.0625`) both failed to improve the K128 anchor, so the mainline loss axis now looks saturated on magnitude.
NOTE: The next clean question is whether the auxiliary dead-feature mechanism is mistimed rather than misweighted; the current threshold `10000000` is effectively inactive over this run length.

NOTE: Judge this run by full curve shape as well as final FVU: if earlier rescue smooths late descent or lowers FVU, the issue was activation timing; if not, the current topk/signum/K128 recipe is likely saturated on the aux-loss path.
r24 topk param_only k128 -> archive fvu=0.15272219479084015
NOTE: Rounds 23 and 24 both regressed the K128 anchor, so the loss-side branch currently looks unpromising without a fresh control run.

NOTE: A full explicit rerun of the exact topk/signum/LR=1.6e-3/K128/EF12 recipe is now the cleanest single-question action because it refreshes the reference curve shape under the current execution stack.

NOTE: Judge this round primarily by reproducibility and late-phase slope: if the clean baseline again descends smoothly into the known FVU band, move next to LR or optimizer decisions with higher confidence; if it drifts materially, treat stack variance as a live factor before drawing more recipe conclusions.
r25 topk param_only k128 -> crash [crash] (first_step_timeout)
